name: HEXAD User Types (Marczewski)
overview: The Gamification User Types Hexad is a typology proposed by Andrzej Marczewski as an extension of Bartle's player types specifically for gameful (non-game) systems. It defines six user types: Philanthropist, Socialiser, Free Spirit, Achiever, Player, and Disruptor. The first four are intrinsically motivated by Relatedness, Autonomy, Mastery, and Purpose (RAMP from Self-Determination Theory and Daniel Pink's Drive). Player is extrinsically motivated by rewards. Disruptor seeks change in the system itself. Each user is a continuous mix of all six rather than a single fixed label.
originator_or_company: Andrzej Marczewski (independent gamification consultant, UK; Gamified UK). Empirical scale development led by Gustavo F. Tondello, Lennart E. Nacke and the HCI Games Group at the University of Waterloo, with later short-form (Hexad-12) work by Krath et al. (2023).
key_resources: [HEXAD: A Player Type Framework for Gamification Design (gamified.uk)](https://www.gamified.uk/user-types/)
[Tondello et al. 2016 - The Gamification User Types Hexad Scale (CHI PLAY '16)](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/2967934.2968082)
[Tondello et al. 2019 - Empirical validation of the Hexad scale in English and Spanish (IJHCS)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1071581918306001)
[Krath et al. 2023 - Hexad-12: Developing and Validating a Short Version (CHI '23)](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3544548.3580968)
[Marczewski - Even Ninja Monkeys Like to Play / The Gamification Design Handbook](https://www.amazon.com/Gamification-Design-Handbook-Ninja-Monkeys/dp/B0C6VV829F)
[HCI Games Group - The Gamification User Types Hexad](https://hcigames.com/user-types-hexad/)
[Marczewski 2013 - A New Perspective on the Bartle Player Types for Gamification](https://www.gamification.co/2013/08/12/a-new-perspective-on-the-bartle-player-types-for-gamification/)
core_mechanics: HEXAD models user motivation along six orthogonal dimensions, scored continuously rather than categorically. INTRINSIC TYPES: (1) Philanthropist - motivated by Purpose/Meaning; altruistic, wants to help others without expectation of reward; mechanics: gifting, mentoring, knowledge sharing, stewardship roles, peer support, narrative meaning. (2) Socialiser - motivated by Relatedness; seeks interaction and belonging; mechanics: teams, guilds, social presence indicators, social comparison, group quests, chat/comments. (3) Free Spirit - motivated by Autonomy; wants to create, explore, self-express; mechanics: branching paths, sandbox/exploration, customization, easter eggs, unlockables, creative tools. (4) Achiever - motivated by Mastery/Competence; wants to learn and overcome challenges; mechanics: levels, progression, challenges, certificates, quests, learning content, boss fights. EXTRINSIC TYPE: (5) Player - motivated by external rewards; will do whatever earns the prize; mechanics: points, XP, badges, leaderboards, virtual currency, lotteries, prizes, FOMO mechanics. DISRUPTIVE TYPE: (6) Disruptor - motivated by change; wants to influence or break the system. Marczewski defines four Disruptor sub-types: Griefer (negative impact on others), Destroyer (breaks the system), Influencer (changes how others use it), Improver (constructive system change); mechanics: voting/feedback channels, anonymity, innovation platforms, development tools, alpha/beta testing access. SCALE: The validated Hexad-24 questionnaire (Tondello 2016) measures 4 items per type with a 7-point Likert scale; users yield a profile with 6 scores (each 4-28). The Hexad-12 (Krath 2023) is a short form with 2 items per type that outperforms the original on model fit. POSITIONAL STRUCTURE: Marczewski places the types around a hexagon. The 'inner circle' (Achiever, Socialiser, Player) operates inside the formal system; the 'outer arc' (Philanthropist, Free Spirit, Disruptor) operates outside formal constraints. Confirmed oppositions: Socialiser-Free Spirit (r approx -0.44), Achiever-Philanthropist, Player-Disruptor.
designer_or_researcher_voice: Marczewski (gamified.uk): 'My User Types are based on inspiration from Bartle's Work but actually come from the four motivators in RAMP - Relatedness, Autonomy, Mastery and Purpose.' Marczewski (Centrical interview): 'Think about behaviours, not types' - cautioning that practitioners should design for motivational behaviours rather than treating types as fixed labels. Marczewski (gamified.uk on Disruptors): 'Disruptors ignored become destructive. Systems with no legitimate route for constructive challenge push that energy underground.' Tondello et al. (CHI PLAY 2016): 'Our survey aims to use a common vocabulary' rather than asking users directly about game elements, because 'users are not necessarily gamers and might not be aware of their game preferences or be familiar with game design vocabulary.' Tondello et al. (2019, IJHCS): 'Each user typically displays a combination of motivations in varying degrees and should not be viewed as a simple categorization system.' Krath et al. (CHI 2023): 'Hexad-12 outperforms the original Hexad scale regarding model fit, reliability, convergent, and discriminant validity.'
metrics_or_evidence: Distribution of dominant single types in Tondello / Marczewski population samples: Philanthropist 23.6%, Achiever 22.7%, Free Spirit 22.4%, Player 18.6%, Socialiser 11.2%, Disruptor 1.6%. Approximately 47% of test-takers cannot be assigned a single dominant type (multiple ties). Mean trait scores (out of normalized scale) hover near: Achiever ~18.4, Free Spirit/Philanthropist ~18.2, Player ~17.6, Socialiser ~16.6, Disruptor ~11.1. Player score declines with age (18.3 under-17 to 15.9 at 60+); Achiever stable across ages. Validation: Tondello 2016 (n=~500) confirmed factor structure and acceptable Cronbach's alpha; Tondello 2019 cross-language replication in English+Spanish (n>900); Krath 2023 Hexad-12 EFA (n=882) and CFA (n=1,101) showed superior fit. Significant correlations exist with Big Five (e.g., Philanthropist with Agreeableness, Achiever with Conscientiousness). Validated translations exist for Brazilian Portuguese, Turkish, German, and Hong Kong Chinese populations. [uncertain] Specific effect sizes of HEXAD-tailored gamification on learning retention vs one-size-fits-all designs are reported as 'small to moderate' in meta-analytic reviews but exact figures vary by study.
criticisms: Theoretical: HEXAD lacks a single coherent psychological theory of origin - it blends Bartle, Self-Determination Theory and Pink's Drive without formal proof of dimensional independence. The Disruptor subscale shows the weakest psychometric stability across language validations. Practical: 47% of users cannot be assigned a single dominant type, undermining the typological framing; Marczewski himself advises 'think about behaviours, not types.' The 24-item scale increases dropout rates and screen fatigue in online surveys (addressed by Hexad-12). Empirical: Effect sizes for HEXAD-personalized gamification vs generic gamification are typically small to moderate and often domain-overridden by content motivation (especially in health/sustainability). Self-Determination Theory researchers note that extrinsic 'Player' rewards risk Overjustification effect, eroding intrinsic motivation in Achievers and Free Spirits over time. Profiles also shift within ~6 months, so static onboarding surveys go stale.
failure_cases: [uncertain] Specific publicly-documented product failures attributed to HEXAD-tailored gamification are scarce; the framework is mainly used in research and consulting rather than at-scale consumer products. Generic gamification failures often cited alongside HEXAD discussions: Khan Academy reducing emphasis on badges/points after evidence of motivation crowding-out; Foursquare demoting mayorships and badges after engagement plateaued; Habitica/Habit RPG retention issues with Players who optimize away from underlying habits once rewards are exhausted. In educational gamification literature, several studies report that ecological/personalized designs only positively affect specific subtypes (e.g., Disruptors' usability, Players' reward perception) while showing null effects for others - suggesting the framework's predictive power is uneven.
antipatterns: (1) Adding extrinsic points or badges to altruistic Philanthropist mechanics (gifting, mentoring) - this triggers the Overjustification effect and can collapse the helping behaviour entirely. (2) Designing only for Achiever and Player because their mechanics (XP, badges, leaderboards) are easiest to implement; produces shallow engagement and excludes ~40% of users. (3) Treating HEXAD scores as a fixed label assigned at onboarding; profiles drift, ties are common, and over-segmenting can deny content to users. (4) Ignoring Disruptors instead of providing a legitimate 'Improver' channel (feedback, beta access, voting) - their energy then goes to griefing or churn. (5) Cosmetic 'fake autonomy' for Free Spirits where all paths converge to the same outcome. (6) Using HEXAD as cover for Dark Patterns: variable-ratio Player rewards, social-pressure Socialiser streaks, FOMO loops - these can produce regulatory exposure (EU DSA, manipulative-design rules) and erode user autonomy. (7) Forcing the long Hexad-24 survey at signup, increasing onboarding dropout.
applicability_to_shikaq: HEXAD provides a useful design lens for shikaq's persona (20s women, career-aspiring, iPad-centric, monthly subscription). Direct mapping to shikaq features: ACHIEVER -> existing Practice/Summary loops with mastery rings (Apple Fitness style), certification badges per resource (IT Pass / FE / SPI / Boki), and the planned Playground for FE科目B coding mastery. PHILANTHROPIST -> AI Q&A 'help-the-next-learner' contributions (anonymized question donations to the corpus), peer commentary on lessons, role-tagging admins. FREE SPIRIT -> non-linear lesson navigation, customizable Today screen, Audio mode for night listening, multiple ways to study (Practice, CBT, AI Q&A), branched curricula. SOCIALISER -> intentionally light-touch given Apple HIG restraint and the persona's solo-cafe study habit; consider opt-in cohorts (e.g., 'study with friends taking 簿記2級 in October') and quiet presence indicators rather than visible chat/leaderboards. PLAYER -> Apple-style restraint: streaks, daily ring closure, subscription-tier perks (rather than gambling-style variable rewards). DISRUPTOR -> in-app feedback/voting on AI-generated questions ('this explanation is wrong'), beta channel for Playground, content-suggestion form. Operationalize via a short Hexad-12 questionnaire on day 7 (not at signup) to lightly tilt the Today recommendation - never to lock content. Apple HIG fit is high because HEXAD informs *what to suggest*, not visual chrome.
phase_assignment: Phase 2 (4-6 weeks). Phase 1 should ship the unpersonalized core (Today / Learn / Practice / Summary) with universally-acceptable Achiever+Free Spirit mechanics. Phase 2 introduces a Hexad-12 lite questionnaire and tilts Today recommendations + nudge tone per dominant trait. Full per-type UI variants (Phase 3) only if Phase 2 metrics justify the complexity.
integration_points: Database: new table user_hexad_profile (user_id FK auth.users, philanthropist int, socialiser int, free_spirit int, achiever int, player int, disruptor int, measured_at timestamptz, version text). Optional questionnaire_responses table for Hexad-12 raw answers. Components: TodayContent.tsx (vary nudge copy and recommended next action per dominant trait), Sidebar.tsx (surface Disruptor 'Send feedback' link more prominently for high-Disruptor users), Practice screen (Achiever-leaning users see harder-tier streaks, Free Spirit-leaning users see explore/random mode), AIQA screen (Philanthropist contribution prompt: 'Donate this Q&A to help others'), Playground (Disruptor beta opt-in). Routes: new /onboarding/hexad route shown on day 7. Analytics: log dominant-type assignment for Sentry/Mixpanel cohort splits.
risks: (1) Persona mismatch: 20s women cafe-study persona may not want overt 'gamer' framing; HEXAD's Player mechanics (points/badges) clash with Apple-style restraint and could feel infantilizing. Mitigation: keep mechanics implicit (rings, streaks) rather than badge-laden. (2) Survey friction: forcing Hexad-12 at signup harms conversion; mitigate by deferring to day 7 and keeping it skippable. (3) Stale profiles: trait scores drift in ~6 months; need re-measurement cadence. (4) Overfitting to dominant type: 47% of users tie across types; force-segmenting denies content. Treat scores as continuous tilts, not gates. (5) Ethical: tilting Disruptor users into more engagement loops is a Dark Pattern risk; keep the feedback channel about *improving the product*, not about retention. (6) Engineering scope creep: per-type UI branches multiply test surface; cap variants to 1-2 dimensions per screen. (7) Evidence quality: real lift over generic design is small-to-moderate, so payoff may not justify complexity if Phase 1 metrics already meet retention targets.
apple_hig_compatibility: needs_adaptation. HEXAD as a *design lens* (which motivation am I addressing?) is fully compatible with Apple HIG's Clarity / Deference / Depth ethos. However, several canonical HEXAD mechanics conflict with the project's Apple-all-in stance: visible leaderboards, point counters, and badge walls violate the 'less but better' principle and the Apple Fitness style (which expresses progress as ambient rings, not explicit numeric scoreboards). Adaptation: implement Player-type mechanics as ambient streaks and ring-closure rather than overt point counts; Socialiser mechanics as quiet presence indicators rather than chat-driven leaderboards; Achiever mechanics as Apple Fitness 3-ring metaphors (problems / accuracy / time) already in shikaq's design. SF Symbols cover the iconography (flame.fill, trophy.fill, person.2.fill, sparkles, hand.raised.fill) without third-party assets.
ethical_score: neutral. HEXAD itself is descriptive and ethically neutral - it is a measurement of motivation, not a manipulation technique. However, applied implementations can swing positive or negative. Positive: respecting Free Spirit autonomy and Philanthropist purpose aligns with Center for Humane Technology / SDT principles; allows users to engage in the way that fits them rather than being forced into a single Player loop. Negative: when used to optimize retention via Player loops (variable rewards, FOMO streaks, Socialiser-pressure mechanics), HEXAD becomes a pretext for Dark Patterns and can drive burnout, time regret, and dependency. Burnout risk specifically high for Achiever users on streak-based daily quotas; time regret risk high for Player users on skinnerian rewards. Net assessment depends entirely on which mechanics are picked - shikaq's Apple-restraint default keeps the implementation on the positive side.
evidence_quality: observational_study (with some company_case_study). The original Hexad framework (Marczewski 2015) is theoretical/practitioner work. The validated scales (Tondello 2016, 2019; Krath 2023) are rigorous psychometric studies with EFA/CFA on samples of ~500-1,100. However, evidence that HEXAD-tailored gamification *outperforms* generic gamification on real outcomes (retention, learning gains) is mostly observational with small-to-moderate effects, not RCTs. No large-scale rigorous_RCT of HEXAD personalization in commercial learning products is publicly known.
localization_fit_japan: medium. Positives: the framework is culture-neutral (motivations are universal), and Japanese learners are familiar with achievement and certification frames. The Achiever+Free Spirit+Philanthropist combination fits the shikaq target persona well. Concerns: (1) No published Japanese-language Hexad-12 validation as of 2026-04 [uncertain] - Brazilian Portuguese, Turkish, German, and Hong Kong Chinese versions exist but a JP psychometric validation is not visible in literature. (2) Japanese 20s women in the cafe-study segment are typically conflict-averse and prefer non-competitive framings; explicit Player mechanics (leaderboards, public scores) likely underperform vs ambient streak / ring designs. (3) Socialiser mechanics need careful handling - public study-group features land differently than in Western markets; opt-in 'quiet co-presence' (similar to Apple SharePlay or LINE Open Chat private rooms) fits better than visible peer competition. Net: framework is usable, but mechanic selection must be biased toward intrinsic types and away from extrinsic comparison.
